description: The provided image displays a system interface element labeled 'Check for updates' with a bookmark icon, which does not contain discernible materials science content, micrographs, or experimental data. The image appears to be a placeholder or incorrectly loaded file, rendering all scientific details unreadable from the visual content. However, based on the accompanying text extraction summary, the paper investigates a low-density steel (Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B wt.%) achieved through hypereutectic TiB₂ and high-Al ferrite phase design. The material exhibits a heterogeneous triplex microstructure comprising 62% equiaxed austenite (3.9 μm grain size), 26% elongated δ-ferrite bands, and 12.5% TiB₂ particles (2.4–35 μm). Key properties include density of 6.88 g cm⁻³, Young's modulus of 225 GPa, yield strength of 503 MPa, and ultimate tensile strength of 672 MPa with 13% elongation. The microstructure characterization involved SEM, EBSD, TEM, synchrotron XRD, and micro-CT, revealing coherent δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interfaces and incoherent austenite/TiB₂ boundaries. The heterogeneous architecture promotes strain partitioning and enhanced specific modulus (32.7 GPa g⁻¹ cm³).

Figure 1 presents the computational alloy design and processing routes for a novel Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B (wt.%) low-density steel. Panel (a) displays equilibrium phase volume fractions versus temperature calculated using Thermo-Calc software with the TCFE9 database, revealing that the nominal composition was intentionally designed to achieve approximately 14 vol.% TiB₂ (stable between 600-1200°C, yellow curve) embedded in a dual-phase matrix of δ-ferrite (initially formed directly from liquid below ~1100°C, gray curve) and austenite (stable between ~650-950°C, blue curve), with complete liquefaction above ~1400°C (red curve). Panel (b) schematically illustrates the fabrication workflow: vacuum induction melting of a 50 kg ingot, casting, homogenization at 1373 K for 2 hours, forging, and subsequent hot rolling in the austenite-ferrite two-phase region through five rolling passes with a finishing temperature of 1173 K to produce a 3 mm thick sheet, followed by air cooling to room temperature. This integrated approach establishes the theoretical foundation for creating a heterogeneous triplex microstructure combining hypereutectic TiB₂ particles, high-Al δ-ferrite bands, and austenite, enabling the steel to simultaneously achieve low density (6.88 g cm⁻³) and high Young's modulus (225 GPa).

Figure 2 presents the finite element simulation set-ups for the micromechanical analysis of the Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B low-density steel. Panel (a) shows the EBSD-based representative volume element (RVE) with dimensions of 200×200 μm², depicting the three-phase microstructure: austenite (blue), δ-ferrite (red), and TiB₂ (yellow). The boundary conditions for periodic loading are indicated by nodal sets a-b (up-down) and c-d (left-right), with reference points RP-1 (loading) and RP-2 (fixed). Panel (b) illustrates the 2D finite element model architecture showing the layered arrangement of γ-Fe, TiB₂, and δ-Fe phases with their respective phase boundaries (PB). Panel (c) provides a close-up view of the local finite-element meshes, revealing the 0.4 μm pixel-based meshing (500×500 elements) automatically generated from EBSD image pixels. This RVE configuration employs CPE4 elements and periodic boundary conditions to simulate macroscopic mechanical responses, stress partitioning among phases, and damage evolution, validating against experimental stress-strain curves and dislocation density measurements.

This figure presents comprehensive mechanical and physical property characterization of the developed low-density steel (LDS, Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B) compared against conventional lightweight steels. Panel (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves where the present LDS (red curve) achieves superior mechanical properties with a yield strength of 503 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 672 MPa, and uniform elongation of 13%, representing 22% and 30% improvements in strength over the conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel (orange curve: 389 MPa yield strength, 549 MPa UTS, 11% elongation) without sacrificing ductility, while the interstitial-free (IF) steel (black curve) shows lower strength but higher elongation. Panel (b) maps specific yield strength (SYS) versus uniform elongation (UE), positioning the LDS (red star) in the optimal upper-right quadrant among diverse alloy systems including Fe-Mn-Ti-B, Fe-Ti-B-(Nb), Fe-Cr-(Ni)-B, and Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. Panel (c) plots specific modulus against the product of SYS and UE, demonstrating that the LDS achieves a high specific modulus of 32.7 GPa g⁻¹ cm³ alongside favorable mechanical performance indices, distinguishing it from both ceramic-reinforced steels and traditional low-density steels. Panel (d) provides the comprehensive legend identifying the various lightweight steel categories compared. The figure collectively establishes that the heterogeneous dual-phase microstructure design enables the LDS to overcome the typical strength-ductility trade-off of ceramic-reinforced steels while maintaining exceptional specific stiffness comparable to light metals.

This figure presents a comprehensive multiscale characterization of the initial microstructure of a hot-rolled Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B (wt.%) low-density steel, focusing on TiB₂ particle distributions and matrix phases. Panel (a) displays a pseudo-3D SEM image showing primary (coarse, polygonal) and eutectic (fine, lamellar) TiB₂ particles distributed across RD, TD, and ND surfaces. High-magnification SEM in (b) reveals hexagonal prism-shaped primary TiB₂ particles on the RD surface, while (c) distinguishes the dual-phase matrix via Mn-rich austenite and Al-rich δ-ferrite regions with corresponding EDS mapping. XRD pattern (d) confirms three phases: δ-ferrite (bcc), austenite (fcc), and hexagonal TiB₂ with indexed peaks. Micro-CT reconstructions in (e) illustrate the 3D spatial distribution of particles across size domains from 0.8–5.0 μm to 20.0–42.7 μm. Statistical histograms (f) and (g) reveal a critical disparity: small particles (0.8–5 μm) constitute ~45% of the number fraction but only ~1% of the volume fraction, whereas coarse particles (10–42.7 μm) occupy >80% of the volume despite lower number density. This bimodal distribution confirms that coarse primary TiB₂ particles, inherited from solidification and retained after hot rolling, dominate the microstructural volume fraction within the ferrite-austenite dual-phase matrix.

This figure presents comprehensive EBSD (Electron Backscatter Diffraction) characterization of the initial microstructure of a novel low-density steel (Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B) processed by hot rolling. The figure comprises pseudo-3D stereographic views (a-a2) and 2D planar maps (b-b2, c-c2) alongside quantitative distributions (b3, c3). Subfigures (a) and (b) display Inverse Pole Figure (IPF) maps revealing grain orientations relative to the rolling direction (RD), transverse direction (TD), and normal direction (ND). Phase maps (a1, b1) identify three constituents: austenite (γ, blue), δ-ferrite (red), and TiB2 particles (yellow), showing a characteristic banded microstructure with alternating δ-ferrite lamellae (thickness ~4.3 μm) and austenite layers (~12.1 μm) aligned along RD. Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps (a2, b2) visualize strain distribution, indicating higher dislocation densities (green/yellow regions) within δ-ferrite bands compared to austenite. Grain size distribution histograms (b3) quantify average grain sizes of 3.9 μm for equiaxed austenite and 4.4 μm for elongated δ-ferrite. Grain Orientation Spread (GOS) maps (c, c1, c2) distinguish recrystallized from deformed grains, with corresponding frequency plots (c3) revealing that 90.8% of austenite grains are fully recrystallized (GOS < 2°) versus only 52.2% of δ-ferrite grains, indicating sluggish recrystallization kinetics in the ferrite phase during hot rolling. This heterogeneous triplex microstructure, comprising partially recrystallized δ-ferrite, fully recrystallized austenite, and ceramic reinforcements, establishes the structural basis for the steel's enhanced strain hardening and mechanical properties.

Bright-field (BF) and weak-beam bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showing dislocation morphologies within the hot-rolled microstructure of a low-density Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B steel containing δ-ferrite (BCC), austenite (FCC), and TiB₂ particles. Subfigures (a), (a₁), and (a₂) focus on δ-ferrite: (a) shows dense dislocation tangles (red arrows) in δ-ferrite grains distant from TiB₂ particles (SAED inset along BCC <001> zone axis, 100 nm scale bar); (a₁) reveals dislocation pile-ups at the δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interface (200 nm scale bar); (a₂) displays detailed dislocation tangles at the same interface under weak-beam conditions with g = 101 diffraction vector (SAED inset, 200 nm scale bar). Subfigures (b), (b₁), and (b₂) focus on austenite: (b) shows sparse dislocation distributions (yellow arrows) in austenite grains away from TiB₂ particles (SAED inset along FCC <011> zone axis, 100 nm scale bar); (b₁) exhibits dislocation pile-ups at the austenite/TiB₂ interface (marked by blue line, 100 nm scale bar); (b₂) shows substantially sparser dislocation tangles at the same interface under weak-beam conditions with g = 11̄1 diffraction vector (SAED inset, 100 nm scale bar). The images demonstrate higher dislocation densities in δ-ferrite compared to austenite, and denser dislocation accumulation at δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interfaces than at austenite/TiB₂ interfaces, consistent with the existence of a coherent/semi-coherent orientation relationship ({110}δ || {1101}TiB₂, <111>δ || <11̄20>TiB₂) at the former but not the latter.

Figure 7 presents combined EBSD and TEM bright-field analysis of a low-density steel (Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B system) after tensile straining. Panels (a-a2) display pseudo-3D stereographic EBSD microstructures: (a) an inverse pole figure (IPF) map showing crystallographic orientations along RD, TD, and ND; (a1) a phase map distinguishing austenite (red), δ-ferrite (blue), and TiB2 particles (yellow); and (a2) a kernel average misorientation (KAM) map indicating local strain distribution from low (blue) to high (green) misorientation. Panels (b-b2) show TEM bright-field images of the BCC δ-ferrite phase: (b) a grain interior distant from TiB2 particles with corresponding BCC <110> selected area diffraction pattern (SADP); (b1) extensive dislocation pile-ups and dense tangles at the δ-ferrite/TiB2 interface; and (b2) the corresponding interface SADP (g=0-11). Panels (c-c2) depict the FCC austenite phase: (c) a grain interior away from TiB2 with FCC <001> SADP; (c1) dislocation pile-ups with sparse tangles at the austenite/TiB2 interface; and (c2) the corresponding interface SADP (g=-111). The images reveal significantly higher dislocation accumulation and denser tangles at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces compared to austenite/TiB2 interfaces, supporting the synergistic deformation mechanism where δ-ferrite provides a stable strengthening framework while austenite accommodates plasticity through dislocation activities.

This figure comprehensively illustrates damage mechanisms in a low-density Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B steel with hypereutectic TiB₂ reinforcements. Pseudo-in-situ SEM micrographs (a-a₃) document microstructural evolution at tensile strains of 0%, 0.7%, 5%, and 9%, where red arrows indicate particle/matrix decohesion and blue arrows denote TiB₂ cracking. Quantitative SEM-EBSD analyses (a₄, a₅) compare crack and decohesion numbers versus engineering strain between FCC (austenite) and BCC (δ-ferrite) matrices, revealing higher crack frequencies in FCC regions. Cross-sectional views (b-b₂) near the fracture surface show crack propagation pathways: large primary TiB₂ particles fracture perpendicular to the loading direction (blue arrows) while interface debonding occurs extensively (red arrows). Fractography (c, c₁) displays mixed-mode failure featuring ductile dimples, fractured TiB₂ particles, and decohesion sites. EDS mapping confirms elemental distributions of Fe, Mn, Al, Ti, and B. These results demonstrate that damage progresses via stress-driven fracture of coarse TiB₂ particles (obeying Weibull statistics) and interfacial decohesion, critically influencing the steel's tensile response.

This figure presents local von Mises strain distributions in a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel containing TiB₂ particles and δ-ferrite phase during tensile deformation. Panel (a) shows the initial EBSD phase map with austenite (γ), δ-ferrite (δ), and TiB₂ particles prior to deformation, where arrows indicate analysis paths. Panels (b)–(f) display strain maps at macroscopic strains of 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%, and 15%, respectively, revealing strain concentration at TiB₂/matrix interfaces oriented approximately 45° to the loading axis (RD), with color scale ranging from 0.00 (blue) to 0.20 (red). Panels (g)–(i) quantify local strain profiles along specific paths: (g) across small TiB₂/δ-ferrite boundaries showing ~10% strain concentration; (h) across matrix/TiB₂ interfaces of various sizes showing elevated strain (~16%) at austenite/TiB₂ boundaries; and (i) across sharp TiB₂/austenite and TiB₂/δ-ferrite boundaries showing maximum strain concentrations (~20%). The results demonstrate that strain localizes heterogeneously at phase boundaries, with higher accumulated strain in the softer austenite compared to δ-ferrite, indicating strain incompatibility and effective promotion of plastic deformation by TiB₂ particles in the surrounding matrix.

This figure presents synchrotron X-ray diffraction analysis and dislocation density calculations for a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel containing δ-ferrite, austenite, and TiB₂ phases. Panels (a) and (b) display line profiles (K = reciprocal lattice spacing) of undeformed and tensile-fractured samples, respectively, showing diffraction peaks indexed to δ-ferrite (black), austenite (blue), and TiB₂ (red). Panels (c) and (d) present Modified Williamson-Hall (MWH) plots for δ-ferrite and austenite, plotting ΔK versus KC^(1/2), where solid symbols represent undeformed and open symbols represent deformed states. Panel (e) compares calculated dislocation densities (10^15 m^-2) between the two phases before and after straining. The data reveal that austenite exhibits a significant increase in dislocation density from (3±0.7)×10^14 to (2±0.3)×10^15 m^-2 after deformation, while δ-ferrite shows only a modest increase from (1.1±0.2)×10^15 to (1.7±0.7)×10^15 m^-2, indicating heterogeneous plastic deformation partitioning between the soft austenite matrix and the harder δ-ferrite bands.

Figure 11 presents finite element method (FEM) simulation results illustrating the mechanical responses and dislocation density evolutions of a novel low-density steel (Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B) featuring a heterogeneous triplex microstructure of δ-ferrite, austenite, and TiB₂. Panel (a) compares simulated true stress-strain curves under damaged (hollow circles) and undamaged (solid lines) conditions for the present steel (LDS), conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel, and interstitial-free (IF) steel against experimental measurements, validating the constitutive model accuracy. Panel (b) depicts the evolution of dislocation densities in δ-ferrite and austenite phases as a function of true strain, showing good agreement between simulated curves and synchrotron XRD experimental data. Panel (c) illustrates stress partitioning among the three phases against global true stress, revealing that TiB₂ bears the highest stress, followed by δ-ferrite and austenite, with the simulation capturing the sequential yielding where austenite yields first at ~490 MPa while δ-ferrite remains elastic initially, contributing to the superior strength-ductility combination.

This figure presents finite element simulation results showing the evolution of dislocation density distributions and phase-specific strengthening contributions in TiB₂-reinforced steels during tensile deformation. Panels (a) and (b) display simulated dislocation density increment (ρ-ρ₀) maps at global strains of ε=0.0%, 5.0%, and 11.0% for conventional Fe–TiB₂ steel and the present low-density steel (LDS), respectively, with the color scale ranging from 2 to 23×10¹⁴ m⁻². The simulations reveal that the conventional steel develops narrow shear bands oriented at approximately 45° with isolated undeformed zones (highlighted by red circles) indicating stress shielding, whereas the heterogeneous LDS matrix exhibits more uniform dislocation distributions due to microscopic strain partitioning between soft austenite and hard δ-ferrite. Panel (c) shows the calculated true stress-strain curve compared with experimental data, decomposed into volume-fraction-weighted strengthening contributions from austenite (blue), δ-ferrite (red), and elastic load-bearing TiB₂ particles (orange), demonstrating that δ-ferrite provides a constant baseline contribution of approximately 170 MPa while austenite delivers increasing work hardening.

This figure presents a comprehensive comparison between experimental observations and finite element (FE) simulations of TiB₂ particle behavior in a Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B low-density steel during tensile deformation. At 5% global engineering strain, SEM image (a) and digital image correlation (DIC) strain map (a₁) reveal particle fracture events (Particle 1 and 2), which correlate with the plastic equivalent strain (PEEQ) map from FE simulation (a₂). The stress history curves (a₃) track the σ₁₁ evolution for individual particles, marking fracture points. At 7% strain, SEM (b) and DIC (b₁) capture both particle decohesion (Particle 3) and fracture (Particle 4), validated by FE results (b₂), with stress curves (b₃) distinguishing debonding at 5% strain from multiple fracture events during deformation. The schematic (c) illustrates the microstructure evolution mechanism involving TiB₂ particles, δ-ferrite bands, and austenite matrix with dislocation interactions. The figure demonstrates that particle fracture tends to accelerate final specimen failure more than decohesion does, with localized strain concentrations driving damage initiation preferentially in particle agglomerates.
